verses.trans: The powers arise from birth, herbs, mantra, austerity, or samādhi.

commentaries.vivekananda: Patanjali opens Kaivalya Pāda by noting that the extraordinary powers (siddhis) described in Pada 3 can arise through five channels: (1) janma — birth (karma from past lives); (2) oṣadhi — herbs, plants, elixirs; (3) mantra — sacred sound; (4) tapas — austerity and self-discipline; (5) samādhi — meditative absorption. Vivekananda: 'There are many roads to the same destination. Some are born with powers; some get them through mantras or herbs. But only samādhi gives powers that are permanent, safe, and accompanied by wisdom. The other methods may give powers without wisdom — a dangerous combination.'
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This sūtra contextualises the siddhis within the broader spiritual landscape: they are not unique to Yoga but arise in many traditions through different means. The Yoga path — through samādhi — is distinguished by the fact that it produces powers as a natural by-product of liberation rather than as an end in themselves.

verses.trans: The transformation into another species is by the overflow of Nature.

commentaries.vivekananda: The transformation from one type of being to another (jāty-antara-pariṇāma) — as in evolution or spiritual transformation — occurs through the overflow (āpūra) of Nature's potentiality. Vivekananda: 'Evolution is not the discovery of Charles Darwin — the Yogis knew it thousands of years ago. But they understood that evolution goes both ways: downward into matter and upward into spirit. The whole creation is gradually evolving toward the expression of the highest consciousness.' Prakṛti contains the potentiality of all forms; transformation is the unfolding of what was latent.
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This is the Yoga theory of evolution: not random mutation but purposeful unfolding of potentialities already present in Prakṛti. The yogin who understands this can consciously participate in and accelerate their own spiritual evolution.

verses.iast: nimittam aprayojakaṁ prakṛtīnāṁ varaṇa-bhedas tu tataḥ kṣetrikavat
verses.trans: The incidental cause does not impel Nature — it removes the obstacle, like a farmer (removing barriers to water flow).

commentaries.vivekananda: The yogin does not create transformation — they remove the obstacles that prevent Nature's inherent potentialities from manifesting. Like a farmer who digs channels so that water flows naturally to the field, the yogin removes the veils (āvaraṇa) that prevent the higher consciousness from expressing itself. Vivekananda: 'You do not manufacture gold from base metal — you remove the dross covering the gold that was always there. The Self is already perfect. You only remove what covers it.'
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This is one of the most important philosophical points in the Yoga Sūtras: spiritual practice does not create anything new. It removes obstacles. The Puruṣa is already pure, already free — the practice removes the obstructions of ignorance and saṃskāra that prevent this fact from being recognised.

verses.trans: The artificially created minds arise from the I-am-ness (asmitā) alone.

commentaries.vivekananda: Advanced yogins can project multiple simultaneously-functioning minds (nirmāṇa-citta — constructed minds) from the single root of asmitā (ego-consciousness). Vivekananda: 'The great masters can be in many places at once — each projection is a fully functional mind created from the root I-am-ness. The yogin does not fragment — they project, like a lamp that illuminates many spaces simultaneously from one flame.' This underlies traditions of bilocation and simultaneous teaching to multiple students.
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: The nirmāṇa-cittas are not separate personalities but projections of the one asmitā — like branches from a single root. They share the master's consciousness but operate in different locations.

verses.trans: Though the activities differ, the one mind is the director of the many.

commentaries.vivekananda: The many projected minds (4.4) are all directed by the one root mind. Like a puppeteer controlling many puppets, the original citta directs all the nirmāṇa-cittas even as each operates independently. Vivekananda: 'The master is one but his influence is everywhere. This is how the great teachers work — not through physical presence alone but through their projected consciousness.'
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This sūtra addresses what would otherwise be a philosophical problem: if there are many minds, which is the real one? Answer: the one root mind that directs all. The plurality is functional, not ontological.

verses.trans: Of these (minds), that born of meditation is free from (karmic) residue.

commentaries.vivekananda: Among all the means of generating siddhis (birth, herbs, mantra, austerity, samādhi — 4.1), only the mind produced through dhyāna/samādhi is anāśaya — free from karmic residue. The other types of extraordinary minds (born from herbs, mantras, etc.) still carry saṃskāras and can create new karma. Vivekananda: 'The powers gotten through drugs or mantras without wisdom are dangerous. They cannot be controlled, they create new karma, and they eventually trap the one who possesses them. Only samādhi-born powers are safe.'
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This is a critical discriminating statement. The practitioner is warned not to seek powers through any means other than the path of samādhi, because only that path produces powers that are integrated with wisdom and free from karmic consequence.

verses.trans: The karma of the yogin is neither white nor black; that of others is of three kinds.

commentaries.vivekananda: Ordinary people's karma is of three types: white (śukla — virtuous, producing pleasure), black (kṛṣṇa — non-virtuous, producing suffering), or mixed (both). The karma of the yogin — one established in samādhi — is neither white nor black. Vivekananda: 'The fully realised yogin does not create new karma — even virtuous action no longer binds because it is performed without desire for the fruit. He acts, but his actions leave no residue.' This is the Gītā's karma-yoga made complete.
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: Aśukla-akṛṣṇa karma (neither white nor black) is karma that creates no future bondage — the action of complete non-attachment. The yogin acts from pure wisdom and compassion, without the ego-driven desire that makes action stick.

verses.trans: From these (three types of karma) only those latent impressions (vāsanās) manifest whose fruition is possible.

commentaries.vivekananda: From the vast storehouse of accumulated karma across many lives, only those vāsanās (deep latent impressions) that are 'ripe' — aligned with the current conditions for fruition — actually manifest. Not all karma ripens simultaneously. Vivekananda: 'The mind is like an ocean of impressions from countless lives. At any given moment only some are surfacing. The others wait. This is why people suddenly change — a dormant vāsanā has become active.'
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This selective activation of karma explains why external circumstances trigger some tendencies and not others, why certain life conditions awaken latent powers or weaknesses. The yogin works to exhaust all vāsanās rather than merely suppressing the currently active ones.

verses.trans: Even though separated by species, place, and time, there is continuity — because memory and saṃskāra are of the same form.

commentaries.vivekananda: The continuity of karma and saṃskāra across different births, different locations, and different times is explained by the fact that memory (smṛti) and saṃskāra (impression) are of the same essential form. The saṃskāra of a past life becomes the memory-tendency that shapes the current life, even if aeons and entirely different species separate them. Vivekananda: 'The soul carries its entire history. Distance in time or space does not diminish the impressions — they are as fresh in the deep mind as the moment they were formed.'
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This is the philosophical basis for reincarnation in the Yoga system. The continuity is not of a substantial soul-entity but of the causal stream of saṃskāra → karma → saṃskāra, which persists across all changes of body and circumstance.

verses.trans: These (vāsanās) are beginningless — because the will to live is eternal.

commentaries.vivekananda: The vāsanās have no discoverable beginning — they stretch back into beginningless time (anāditva), because the fundamental will to live and to experience (āśiṣ) is itself beginningless. Vivekananda: 'We cannot find the first moment of bondage because there was no first moment. The soul has always been in this dream of mortality — and the Yoga path is how the dreamer wakes up.' This has a liberating implication: we need not trace the origin of suffering, only remove it.
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: The beginninglessness of vāsanās might seem discouraging, but Patanjali means the opposite: since bondage has no starting point, it is not inherently fixed. It is maintained by ongoing ignorance — and ignorance can be removed now, regardless of its duration.

verses.trans: Since these (vāsanās) are held together by cause, result, support, and object — with their absence, these too cease.

commentaries.vivekananda: The vāsanās are maintained by four interdependent factors: hetu (cause — ignorance), phala (result — karmic fruit), āśraya (support — the mind that harbours them), and ālambana (object — the sense-objects that activate them). Remove any one of these and the whole system begins to unravel. Remove all four — through Yoga — and the vāsanās cease entirely. Vivekananda: 'Cut off ignorance, stop feeding desires, purify the mind, and withdraw from sense-objects — do all four and liberation is certain.'
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This is a systems-analysis of bondage: it is maintained by a self-reinforcing loop of cause, effect, support, and stimulation. Yoga attacks all four simultaneously through its eightfold path.

verses.trans: The past and future exist in their own form — the difference (between past, present, future) is due to the difference of the path (adhva) of properties.

commentaries.vivekananda: Past and future are not non-existent — they exist in their own latent or transformed form. The difference between past, present, and future is not ontological (they don't literally disappear) but a difference in which properties are currently manifest (adhva-bheda — difference in the path/stage of properties). Vivekananda: 'Time is not a river that carries things away into nothingness. The past exists — it is simply in a different condition. That is why the yogin can perceive past and future through saṁyama (3.16).'
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This is Patanjali's philosophy of time — often called 'three-temporal realism.' All three times are real; what changes is which properties are actualised. This accounts for both memory (the past leaves real traces) and prediction (the future has real tendencies).

verses.trans: These (properties of past and future) are manifest or subtle — their nature is the guṇas.

commentaries.vivekananda: The properties that characterise the three times are either manifest (vyakta — currently expressed in experience) or subtle (sūkṣma — latent, not yet or no longer expressed). In both cases they are constituted by the three guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas) — all of Prakṛti's expressions, across all times, are modifications of these three. Vivekananda: 'Everything in the universe — past, present, or future — is a play of the three forces: intelligence, activity, and inertia. There is nothing outside them in the realm of matter.'
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This sūtra connects the theory of time (4.12) to the fundamental Sāṃkhya metaphysics: the guṇas are the ultimate constituents of all temporal experience. Time itself is a function of the guṇas' interaction.

verses.trans: The reality (tattva) of a thing is due to the unity of its transformation.

commentaries.vivekananda: Each object has a unified, determinate nature (vastu-tattva) because its transformation is singular — it changes in one integrated way, not randomly. The clay pot has a specific reality because its properties change in a unified, lawful manner. Vivekananda: 'Things are real — the Yoga does not say the world is mere illusion. But their reality is dependent and relational — it is the reality of transforming patterns within Prakṛti, not the ultimate reality of the unchanging Puruṣa.'
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This is Patanjali's response to extreme idealism (which denies external reality) and extreme materialism (which denies the reality of the observer). Objects are genuinely real as patterns of transformation — but their reality is a lower order than that of Puruṣa.

verses.trans: With the same object, different minds perceive differently — hence the two (mind and object) follow different paths.

commentaries.vivekananda: The same external object (vastu) is perceived differently by different minds (citta-bheda). A flower appears beautiful to one person and mundane to another; a flame appears warming to one and threatening to another. This shows that mind and object follow different paths — they are not identical. Vivekananda: 'The world you see is coloured by your mind. Two people in the same room live in different worlds. This is not subjective idealism — it is the recognition that perception is always mind-object co-creation.'
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This is philosophically important: it argues against the view that objects are purely mental projections (idealism) by noting that different minds perceive the same object differently — proving the object has an existence independent of any particular mind.

verses.dev: न चैकचित्ततन्त्रं वस्तु तदप्रमाणकं तदा किं स्यात् ।
verses.iast: na caika-citta-tantraṁ vastu tad apramāṇakaṁ tadā kiṁ syāt
verses.trans: Nor is an object dependent on one mind — if it were not cognised by that mind, what would it be?

commentaries.vivekananda: Objects are not dependent on any single mind for their existence. If they were, they would cease to exist when that mind ceased to cognise them. But objects are experienced by many minds and persist across time. Vivekananda: 'The tree in the forest does not disappear because no one is looking at it. Objects have their own reality — independent of any individual mind, though not independent of the Cosmic Mind (Puruṣa / Brahman) that underlies all.' Patanjali steers between solipsism and materialism.
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This sūtra, combined with 4.15, establishes a middle position: objects are real but mind-coloured. Neither purely objective (the same to all minds) nor purely subjective (existing only for one mind).

verses.trans: An object is known or unknown according to whether the mind is coloured by it or not.

commentaries.vivekananda: An object becomes known when the mind (citta) is coloured or influenced (uparāga) by it — when there is contact between the object and the mind. Objects exist independently but are perceived only when the mind turns toward them and takes on their colouring. Vivekananda: 'Knowledge requires both the object and the knowing mind. Neither alone is sufficient. When the mind's colour merges with the object's colour, knowledge arises.' The clarity of knowledge depends on the purity of the mind.
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: Uparāga (colouring, reflection) is the key term. The mind is like a crystal — it takes on the colour of what it reflects. The object exists; perception is the mind reflecting the object's form. This model applies all the way to self-knowledge: the mind reflects the Puruṣa and that reflection is called self-awareness.

verses.iast: sadā jñātāś citta-vṛttayas tat-prabhoḥ puruṣasya apariṇāmitvāt
verses.trans: The modifications of the mind are always known by their master — the Puruṣa — because of Its unchangingness.

commentaries.vivekananda: All the mind's modifications (vṛttis) are always known to the Puruṣa — the ever-present witnessing consciousness — because the Puruṣa itself never changes. It is the unchanging witness against which all changes are perceived. Vivekananda: 'You are always aware of your thoughts — even when you are not thinking about being aware. This constant awareness, which never goes out, is the Soul. The soul watches the mind the way the sun illuminates the earth — without itself being affected by what it illuminates.'
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This establishes the epistemological foundation of the whole system: the Puruṣa as the ever-present witness (sākṣin) whose unchangingness is what makes change knowable. Without an unchanging witness, change could not be perceived as change.

verses.trans: The mind is not self-luminous — because it is an object (of perception).

commentaries.vivekananda: The mind is not self-luminous (svābhāsa) — it does not know itself by itself. It is dṛśya (an object of perception) — seen by the Puruṣa. Vivekananda: 'The mind is not consciousness — it is matter. It can think only because it reflects the light of the Soul. By itself it is as dark and inert as a stone. The light of the Soul makes it seem as if the mind is conscious.' This crucial distinction prevents the mind from being confused with the Self.
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This is a direct refutation of the Buddhist view (as Patanjali understood it) that consciousness is a product of the mind. For Yoga, the mind is Prakṛti — unconscious matter — and consciousness (Puruṣa) is an entirely separate reality that illuminates the mind.

verses.trans: And (the mind cannot know) both (itself and the object) at the same time.

commentaries.vivekananda: The mind cannot simultaneously know itself (as knower) and know an external object (as known) in the same moment. This proves it is not self-luminous: a truly self-luminous consciousness (like Puruṣa) can be aware of itself and of its objects simultaneously. The mind, being limited, can only attend to one thing at a time. Vivekananda: 'Watch how the mind works: when you are absorbed in an object, you forget yourself. When you reflect on yourself, the object fades. The mind cannot do both at once. The Soul, however, always knows everything.'
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This logical argument confirms that the mind is an instrument, not consciousness itself. Its sequential nature (first this, then that) proves it cannot be the ultimate knower.

verses.trans: If the mind were cognised by another mind, there would be infinite regress and confusion of memory.

commentaries.vivekananda: If the mind were known by another mind — and that by yet another — there would be an infinite regress (atiprasaṅga) of knowers. Additionally, memory would become confused — which mind's memory belongs to which mind? Vivekananda: 'This logical argument shows that there must be one ultimate, self-luminous knower that is not itself known by anything else. That is the Puruṣa — consciousness knowing itself, requiring no external knower.'
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This is a philosophical argument for the necessity of Puruṣa as the ultimate witness. The regress problem (who knows the knower?) is solved only by positing a self-luminous consciousness that is its own light — Puruṣa.

verses.trans: The unchanging consciousness (Puruṣa), appearing to take the form of the intellect, achieves cognition of its own intellect.

commentaries.vivekananda: The Puruṣa is apratisaṃkrama — it does not literally move or migrate into the mind. But when the mind takes the form of any object, the Puruṣa illuminates that form, and it appears as if the Puruṣa itself has become that form. Thus the Puruṣa 'achieves cognition of its own intellect' — the intellect, lit by Puruṣa's unchanging light, knows itself in reflection. Vivekananda: 'The soul does not move — it is the moveless centre. But by reflecting through the moving mind, it seems to move. Remove this illusion and the Soul is found to be eternally still and eternally aware.'
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This is the most subtle description in the entire Sūtras of how perception works: Puruṣa is motionless; the mind moves and takes on forms; Puruṣa illuminates these forms; this illumination appears as if Puruṣa itself has become the form.

verses.trans: The mind, coloured by both the Seer (Puruṣa) and the seen (objects), is all-comprehending.

commentaries.vivekananda: The mind is uniquely positioned between Puruṣa and the world of objects — coloured by both. It receives the light of Puruṣa (making it conscious-seeming) and reflects the forms of objects (making them known). This dual colouring makes it sarvārtha — comprehensive, able to know all things. Vivekananda: 'The mind is the great mediator — it stands between the soul and the world. Purify it, and it becomes the transparent lens through which the Soul illuminates all of creation.'
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This explains why the purification of citta is central to Yoga: the mind is the only instrument through which Puruṣa and the world can meet. Purify it completely and both are known in their true nature.

verses.iast: tad asaṁkhyeya-vāsanābhiś citram api parārthaṁ saṁhatya-kāritvāt
verses.trans: Though variegated by countless vāsanās, the mind acts for another (the Puruṣa) — because it acts in combination.

commentaries.vivekananda: Though the mind seems to be an independent agent with its own colourful personality (variegated by countless vāsanās), it actually serves another — the Puruṣa. This is because it acts only in combination (saṃhatya-kāritva) — it needs to be illuminated by Puruṣa to function. An independent mind with no consciousness behind it would be inert. Vivekananda: 'The mind is the servant of the soul — though it has forgotten this in its pride. All the mind's complexity and variedness exists for the soul's liberation. Once liberation is achieved, the mind has fulfilled its purpose.'
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This reinforces the teleology of Prakṛti from 2.18 and 2.21: even the mind, in all its complexity, exists for Puruṣa's liberation, not as an end in itself.

verses.trans: For one who has seen the distinction (between Puruṣa and mind), the cultivation of the sense of Self ceases.

commentaries.vivekananda: For one who has clearly seen the distinction (viśeṣa-darśin) between Puruṣa and citta — who has genuine viveka — the habitual tendency to cultivate a sense of self in the mind (ātma-bhāva-bhāvanā) naturally ceases. Vivekananda: 'When you truly know you are not the mind, the mind no longer pulls you into its dramas. You may still have thoughts — but you are no longer convinced by them.' The ego-building process simply stops.
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: Ātma-bhāva-bhāvanā (cultivating the sense of self in the mind) is what ordinary psychology calls ego-reinforcement — the habitual re-identification with the mental personality. Viveka ends this habit, not through suppression but through clear seeing.

verses.trans: Then the mind is inclined toward discrimination and gravitates toward kaivalya.

commentaries.vivekananda: When ātma-bhāva-bhāvanā has ceased (4.25), the mind is freed from its ego-building project and naturally inclines (nimna — slopes downward, as water flows to the lowest point) toward discrimination (viveka) and gravitates toward kaivalya (liberation). Vivekananda: 'When the mind gives up its false independence and recognises itself as the servant of the soul, it naturally flows toward liberation — like a river flowing to the sea. No more effort is needed — just the removal of the dam.'
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: Viveka-nimna (discrimination-inclined) describes the final orientation of the purified mind: it naturally tilts toward discrimination the way water naturally tilts toward the lowest level. This is the penultimate stage.

verses.trans: In the gaps (of this discrimination), other cognitions arise from saṃskāras.

commentaries.vivekananda: Even in this advanced state, saṃskāras from deep in the mind's history can still arise and create distracting cognitions in the gaps of discriminative awareness. Vivekananda: 'Even the greatest yogi, near the end of the path, may find old habits surfacing unexpectedly. This is the last resistance of the unconscious mind. Do not be discouraged — just return to discrimination each time.' The practitioner must not mistake these intrusions for a fall but simply continue.
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: The 'gaps' (chidra) in discrimination are moments of inattention where the witnessing awareness wavers. The saṃskāras rush in immediately. This is why the continuity of viveka-khyāti (aviplavā — 2.26) is so important: no gaps.

verses.trans: Their removal is described as (being) like the kleśas.

commentaries.vivekananda: The removal of these final intrusive saṃskāras (4.27) follows the same process as the removal of the kleśas described in Pada 2. Vivekananda: 'Use the same tools that dissolved the gross kleśas: watchful awareness, non-reaction, tracing them back to their source, and continued discrimination. There is no new method needed at this advanced stage — the same path that brought you here continues to the end.'
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This sūtra beautifully ties Pada 4 back to Pada 2: the entire path is a single continuous process of removing layers of misidentification, from the grossest to the subtlest, using the same tools throughout.

verses.iast: prasaṁkhyāne 'py akusīdasya sarvathā viveka-khyāter dharma-meghaḥ samādhiḥ
verses.trans: For one who shows no interest even in omniscience — being established in total discriminative knowledge in all ways — comes the samādhi of the dharma-cloud.

commentaries.vivekananda: Dharma-megha samādhi — the samādhi of the cloud of dharma (virtue/law). This is the final samādhi before complete liberation: the practitioner has become totally established in viveka-khyāti in all circumstances and shows no interest even in the highest spiritual attainments (prasaṃkhyāna — complete enlightened knowledge). Like a cloud that rains dharma on all below it, this samādhi spontaneously produces virtue, wisdom, and liberation. Vivekananda: 'When a man has given up everything — even the desire for liberation — and stands in complete discrimination with no attachment anywhere, the final samādhi comes like a rain-cloud, flooding everything with wisdom and peace.'
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: Dharma-megha is unique to the Yoga system — not found under this name elsewhere, though it corresponds to high states in Buddhism and Vedānta. It is the threshold state just before kaivalya.

verses.trans: From that (dharma-megha samādhi) comes the cessation of kleśas and karma.

commentaries.vivekananda: The practical fruit of dharma-megha samādhi: all five kleśas are completely extinguished and no new karma is created. Vivekananda: 'This is the state of the Jīvanmukta — the one who is liberated while still living. The kleśas are gone; karma no longer accumulates; the soul acts with perfect freedom while in the body.' The seeds of bondage have been burned; only the fruit already on the vine remains (prārabdha karma) and will exhaust itself naturally.
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This is what the tradition calls jīvanmukti — liberation in life. The body continues; consciousness continues; but the mechanism of bondage is permanently dismantled.

verses.iast: tadā sarva-āvaraṇa-mala-apetasya jñānasya ānantyāj jñeyam alpam
verses.trans: Then, all coverings and impurities having been removed, knowledge is infinite — and what remains to be known is little.

commentaries.vivekananda: When all the coverings (āvaraṇa) and impurities (mala) have been completely removed from consciousness, knowledge becomes infinite (ānantya). And 'what remains to be known is little' — meaning that from the perspective of infinite knowledge, the entire phenomenal world, vast as it seems, is a small thing. Vivekananda: 'The infinite soul, freed from all coverings, can know everything. The universe, which seemed so vast, becomes like a small cloud passing across the infinite sky of consciousness.' This is omniscience not as a collection of facts but as the nature of pure awareness.
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This is the state of Sarvajña — the all-knowing — not encyclopaedic knowledge but the open awareness that contains all possible knowledge because it is prior to all limitation.

verses.trans: Then the sequence of transformations of the guṇas, having fulfilled their purpose, ends.

commentaries.vivekananda: The guṇas have now fulfilled their purpose (kṛtārtha — purpose accomplished) with respect to this Puruṣa. Their sequence of transformations (pariṇāma-krama) — which constituted the entire evolution from ignorance to liberation — comes to an end. Vivekananda: 'Nature has done her work. She escorted the soul from the beginning of creation to the threshold of liberation — and now she steps back. The play is over; the stage dismantles.' For this liberated Puruṣa, Prakṛti ceases to function.
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: This is the Sāṃkhya-Yoga account of what happens to Nature at liberation: it does not disappear cosmically (it continues for other Puruṣas) but its transaction with this particular Puruṣa is complete. The relationship ends.

verses.trans: Sequence (krama) is the correlate of moments (kṣaṇa), apprehended at the end of transformation.

commentaries.vivekananda: Krama (sequence) is defined precisely: it is the moment-by-moment succession of changes that becomes fully apparent only at the end of a transformation — when looking back at the complete arc. Vivekananda: 'Sequence exists only in time. When time ends for the liberated yogin, so does sequence. In kaivalya there is no more before and after — only the eternal present.' This sūtra resolves the earlier discussion of kṣaṇa and krama from 3.52.
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: At the moment of kaivalya, sequence itself — the fundamental structure of all experienced reality — is transcended. This is why the tārakaṁ jñāna of 3.54 is akrama (without sequence): it sees all at once, outside of time.

verses.dev: पुरुषार्थशून्यानां गुणानां प्रतिप्रसवः कैवल्यं स्वरूपप्रतिष्ठा वा चितिशक्तिरिति ।
verses.iast: puruṣārtha-śūnyānāṁ guṇānāṁ pratiprasavaḥ kaivalyaṁ svarūpa-pratiṣṭhā vā citi-śaktir iti
verses.trans: Kaivalya is the return of the guṇas to their origin — now empty of purpose for the Puruṣa — or it is the establishment of the power of consciousness (citi-śakti) in its own nature.

commentaries.vivekananda: The final sūtra: kaivalya is described two ways — (1) from the side of Prakṛti: the guṇas return to their unmanifest source (pratiprasava — reverse evolution), now empty of purpose for this Puruṣa; (2) from the side of Puruṣa: the power of pure consciousness (citi-śakti) is established (pratiṣṭhā) in its own true nature (svarūpa). Vivekananda: 'The soul stands alone — free, pure, all-knowing, all-powerful, beyond time, beyond cause, beyond the universe itself. This is the goal — this is what it means to be truly free. Not freedom from something — but freedom as the very nature of the Soul. This is Kaivalya — this is liberation — this is the final word of Yoga.'
commentaries.patanjali_vyasa: Patanjali closes the Yoga Sūtras with this double description of liberation: from Prakṛti's side it is the dissolution of the purpose-driven evolution; from Puruṣa's side it is eternal self-recognition. The citi-śakti (power of consciousness) — which was always the Puruṣa's nature — is now experienced directly, permanently, without mediation. Iti — thus ends the Yoga Darśana of Patañjali.
